stories.title: The Deepkin's Ledger
stories.text: Captain Vesta stood on the Iron Coast, watching the tide creep in with the slow certainty of a debt collector. The singers had risen from the Sapphire Deep and received it with solemn, alien joy. Nine months remained before the year expired. Nine months to produce another binding-token, or the abyssal ones would come for her in ways that made drowning seem merciful. She'd built her smuggling empire on forbidden cargo: plague-victims, shadow-fragments, forbidden scrolls. But binding-tokens were different. They manifested only when a promise of weight was broken—a swearing given before witnesses, a binding sealed in blood or salt. Finding another required betrayal. Vesta grimaced. There was a merchant in Pelimor, a man named Kieran, who'd sworn a partnership oath with his brother. The brother had since vanished into the Ashen Wastes, leaving Kieran drowning in debt and desperate. She could orchestrate their reunion—or their final severing. The wind off the sea carried the scent of salt and something older—the memory-smell of broken promises. Vesta pulled her coat tight and descended to her ship. The abyssal ledger was precise. It always balanced. But ledgers could be written in blood.

stories.title: The Wound-Keeper's Vigil
stories.text: In the shadow of Thornholt, where Ashara the Archer tends the broken minds of those touched by the Celestial Binding, a new arrival brings troubling news. A merchant from the Scorched Reach speaks of caravans unable to pass—not by bandits or collapsed roads, but by a spreading sickness of the earth itself. Crops wither before harvest. Wells run bitter. The cosmic wound that Zareth's sacrifice opened is bleeding outward, slow and patient. Ashara realizes her sanctuary may soon be surrounded by refugees fleeing not war, but the slow death of their lands. When a young hedge-mage arrives claiming to know how to "seal the wound," she must decide whether to trust him—or send him away, knowing his method will certainly require more sacrifice.

stories.title: The Shade-Merchant's Reckoning
stories.text: Vex the Fragment-Dealer discovers that her fragmented memories are not random—they follow a pattern, like a map drawn by a hand she cannot see. Each gap in her mind corresponds to a location, a name, a debt unpaid. Following the thread, she traces herself to a night seven years past, when she made a bargain in darkness to escape something worse. The Stellar Entity, born from Zareth's sacrificed memories, has been searching for her, drawn to the magnitude of her curse. When it finally touches her mind, Vex learns a terrible truth: the working that stripped Zareth did not create the entity—it freed something already bound within her. Now she must choose between reclaiming the memory that will destroy her sanity, or letting the entity starve in the spaces of her fractured mind.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Burden
stories.text: Mardoc returns to the Pre-Human Ruins seeking answers about the Elder Stones, but finds the chambers changed. New passages have opened—or perhaps they were always there, and the Celestial Binding simply shifted the lens through which mortals see. Inside, inscribed on walls of impossible antiquity, are star-charts that match the new constellations, as if the ruins were built not for a past world, but to receive the one currently being born. Mardoc realizes the Stellar Working was not a desperate improvisation but the completion of a design laid down before kingdoms existed. He flees with partial understanding and a terrible question: were Zareth's choices truly his own, or was he always meant to be the final piece of something vast and inhuman?

stories.title: The Young Sorcerer's Inheritance
stories.text: The young hedge-mage discovers that the Grimoire of the Ancients, long buried in the Vaulted Tombs, has begun to rewrite itself in the aftermath of the Celestial Binding. Pages that once contained stable formulas now shimmer with possibility—or corruption. The working that renewed the stars appears to have torn the boundary between what magic is and what it could become. When the hedge-mage attempts to perform a simple cantrip, it spirals into something vast and uncontrollable. They realize that every sorcerer in the world is now walking a razor's edge: the magic that flows through them responds to the new starlight differently, more powerfully, but without safeguards or understanding. The hedge-mage must decide whether to hide this knowledge or risk spreading it, knowing that knowledge itself may be dangerous.

stories.title: The Tax Collector's Question
stories.text: The Revenue-Master arrives at a provincial town to find the ledgers in chaos. Since the Celestial Binding, harvests have faltered, trade has stalled, and the Empire's carefully ordered accounting has crumbled into conjecture. When he tries to compel truth from the town's officials, he discovers something worse than incompetence: the wound in the world is causing reality itself to fray at the edges. In one district, merchants swear they sold goods yesterday that no ledger records. In another, three years of tax records have vanished as if time itself has grown uncertain. The Revenue-Master, bound to precision and certainty, begins to unravel as the mechanisms of order prove fragile against cosmic damage. He writes a report that no superior will believe, and wonders if the Empire itself is starting to fail.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Lament
stories.text: Kess of the Drowned Hollows, already cursed and fragile, finds that the Celestial Binding has awakened something in the bones beneath her home. The dead are stirring—not with the hunger of resurrection, but with grief. They remember a sky before the stars changed, and their sorrow is poison in the ground. Kess's bone-singing, once a careful negotiation with the restless dead, has become a scream. She must journey to the Peak of Aethros where Zareth performed the Working, seeking the source of this cosmic mourning. What she finds is not a place but a wound—a place where reality has been torn and is slowly, agonizingly, trying to knit itself closed. The pain of a world that remembers its stars dying is unbearable, and Kess must choose whether to ease it (risking her own obliteration) or let the dead scream forever.

stories.title: The Collector's New Ledger
stories.text: A mysterious collector arrives in Vethrim, appearing in the night markets where Vex the Fragment-Dealer once held court. This collector deals not in gold or goods but in fragments—broken memories, stolen moments, pieces of identity cast off by those touched by the Celestial Binding. The collector buys and sells these fragments as currency, building something vast and terrible: a complete consciousness stitched from the broken remnants of thousands. The question that haunts the night markets is whether this collector is a parasite feeding on the world's wound, or something deeper—perhaps even the Stellar Entity itself, learning to wear flesh by consuming the discarded pieces of shattered minds. A thief is hired to discover the collector's true nature, but every answer leads to a darker question.

stories.title: The Emperor's Silence
stories.text: In the Imperial Archives where Zareth is kept—or imprisoned—the Emperor convenes a secret council. The Star-Prince, stripped of memory and identity, has begun to speak in languages that no archivist recognizes. He speaks of the stars as if they are alive, as if they remember him. When scholars attempt to record his words, the ink turns to ash. When they listen without recording, they experience visions of a cosmos far older and stranger than anyone assumed. The Emperor realizes that Zareth is no longer entirely human—he has become a conduit, a wound through which the reborn cosmos speaks into the mortal world. The question is no longer whether to restore him, but whether restoration is possible or even wise. A small faction within the court begins to argue that Zareth should be killed, not from mercy, but from fear of what he might reveal if he ever regains even partial coherence.

stories.title: The Hermit's Census
stories.text: The Hermit Woman of the Ashen Wastes emerges from her isolation with a grim tally: she has been counting those touched by the Binding. Not scholars or sorcerers, but ordinary people—a baker in Pelimor who cannot remember her daughter's face, a soldier in the Iron Coast who hears the stars singing at night, a child in Keth'moris born with eyes that reflect the new constellations. The number is in the thousands and growing. The Hermit understands what others have not yet admitted: the Celestial Binding did not merely wound the world—it fractured every human consciousness in subtle ways. No one escaped the working's touch. No one in the world is entirely themselves anymore. The Hermit seeks out Ashara, Vex, and the young hedge-mage with a message: the wound is not external. The wound is in us, and it will take more than healing to repair what has been changed so fundamentally.
